# vLLM Profiling – Insights for Prefill time and TTFT 

[Ofer Biran](mailto:biranofer@gmail.com), [Evgeny Shindin](mailto:ev.shindin@gmail.com)

Disclaimer: Although this is still a work in progress, we felt that some of the insights could be useful for the LLM-D Autoscaler SIG and worth sharing now.

## Introduction

Assessment of the prefill time and TTFT (Time To First Token) of a vLLM server based on various available metrics is important for both the scheduler and auto-scaler of a vLLM cluster:

* The scheduler can get assessment of replica (a worker running a vLLM server, can be a physical machine, VM or a Kubernetes pod) state according to observable metrics, for the TTFT-related SLO consideration affecting the routing decision.  
* The scheduler load-balancing decisions related to TTFT effect (assess the TTFT effect of additional load on a replica, e.g. whether it is closed to a saturation point).  
* The auto-scaler can assess the overall capacity required of a vLLM cluster to achieve TTFT-related SLO goals, considering the current and predicted requests’ load.  
* The auto-scaler can identify by the observable metrics approaching a saturation point (on a cluster level, combining the individual replicas’ states), and take a proactive scaling action before saturation occurs. 

In this work we tried to reach a *clean* assessment model for the TTFT and prefill time (which is a dominating component of the TTFT). By clean we mean using artificial and deterministic requests in order to pinpoint the effect of each parameter. E.g. experiments where all requests have exactly the same number of input tokens (as opposed to running some chat benchmark data), in order to isolate the effect of the input token length parameter. 

The results show that it’s possible to provide a model for the prefill time in the prefill/decode dis-aggregation mode (I.e. assessing replicas that are only doing prefill), however when prefill & decode are combined on the same replica things get tougher. One of the reasons might be the complex scheduling algorithm in the vLLM scheduler (with waiting, running queues and actually-scheduled batch) that complicates a possible TTFT/prefill modeling.  In this case it’s more practical to try to determine *saturation point* according to the metrics as opposed to providing a full modelling.

Other insights provided are related to the actual meaning of some metrics, absence of quadratic affect in prefill time modeling, comparing client side to server side metrics, effect of prefix cache hit ratio, safe-guarding of the max-num-batch-tokens setup parameter,  separation of requests replica targets by prompt lengths, saturation points for various input token / output token lengths, effect of ITPS (input tokens per second) with different input token lengths (but same ITPS).

## Experiments framework and setup

The experiment framework ‘runs’ an experiment file with parameters for:

* Setup of the vLLM server: version, request concurrency limit, prefix cache, max number batch tokens  
* LLM Model  
* Metric collection: duration (determine the experiment time), interval  
* Load: streaming mode, total RPS, concurrency number of threads generating requests), requests length, max output tokens, prefix cache hit ratio. The last three are specified as lists for a series of sub-experiments multi-looping on the values.

In the provided results here we used the mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct model, L40 and  A100 GPUs, Openshift POD with 40GB memory. 

The framework runs an experiment by: 

* Deploying the vLLM server in an Openshift cluster according the setup parameters  
* Scraping the server metrics according the interval (using the /metric vLLM route) – on Streaming mode collects also the client-side  metrics for TTFT.   
* In each sub-experiment generate the load according the load parameters (assuming Poisson requests arrival, divided between the load worker threads)  
* Produce the metric summaries and time series graphs.

## Prefill time modeling

When there is no waiting requests queue, the prefill time is the dominating service factor for the TTFT  (when such queue is created at saturation point, it’ll become the dominating factor. In prefill-only scenario the prefill time is the cause for the queue).      To correctly model the prefill time the experiments should impose a prefill-only scenario, we get close to this by setting the max output tokens to minimal value of 2 (with a value of 1 there is a problem with some metrics as vllm:num\_requests\_running).

From initial experiments it was clear that the prefill time depends on the number of input tokens \- T (i.e. prompt length) and the number of concurrently running requests in the GPU batch \- NR. For the modelling we considered the affect of their multiplicity NR\*T and quadratic effect of T (NR\*T^2) .   

For the NR and T dependency a linear contribution can be identified:

First for NR, In the following graph each experiment is with a fixed number of input tokens (e.g. in T530 experiments series all requests have 530 input tokens) and a fixed number of concurrently running requests NR (enforced by the max-num-batched-tokens vLLM server parameter) – which are the x axis values:![A graph with different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image1]

Similarly for T in the following graph, where the experiments series are now assembled by the NR and the input tokens T are the x axis values:

![A graph with different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image2]

For the multiplicity NR \* T we can see the following graph which shows a strong linear dependency:![A graph with different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image3]

And for the quadratic contribution of NR \* T^2 we get:

![A graph with different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image4]

Putting it all together in OLS fitting, trying Prefill \= A \+ B\*NR \+ C\*T \+D\*NR\*T \+ E\*NR\*T^2 we get a very good fitting result:

![A screenshot of a computerAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image5]

With the following actual versus predicted graphs (coloring by NR and by T):

![A comparison of a graphAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image6]

An interesting insight here is the absence of major quadratic affect, as was expected considering the prefill task. This can be related to improvements in vLLM prefill as flash attention.

## Prefix cache hit ratio effect

Here we assess the effect of the prefix cache hit ration on the prefill time and TTFT.  We enforced specific hit ratio by assembling the prompt of all requests to start with a prefix equal to that of a first request, whose size is proportional to the required hit ration, and the rest is randomly chosen.  The following graph shows the relation, for experiments series of 1000, 3000 and 5000 input tokens:                                                       ![A graph of different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image7]

From inspecting the metrics of running and waiting requests, it appears that for TTFT the major affect is the ability to increase the number of concurrent requests running du to the GPU memory saving.

A very interesting insight here is that the effecy of the hit ration is equivalent to shorten the request length according to the hit ration. For example, the prefill time and TTFT for requests of 1000 input tokens with hit ration of 0.3 are similar to those for requests of 700 input tokens with no prefix cache (or 0 hit ration). This can be clearly seen in the following graph, where PR-EQ and TTFT-EQ are the equivalent experiments with no prefix cache: 

![A graph of different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image8]

## Validation of client versus server metrics

As we count on the vLLM server /metric rout we wanted to validate the server-side TTFT metric against the client view.  We can see below indeed a constant delay due client network latency I various experiments. PT is number of threads generating requests, TTFT is the server side metric and CL-TTFT is the client side metric.

![A collage of graphsAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image9]

## Prediction of saturation point

As stated above, providing accurate modeling to TTFT when prefill and decode tasks are combined is difficult due to the complex scheduling inside the vLLM scheduler.  So instead we’re trying to determine *saturation point* according to the metrics.

In the following we use these terms:

* RPS – Request Per Seconds  
* MaxTinB \- max-num-batched-tokens  
* T2000 – run with all request 2000 input tokens  
* OT – num of output tokens  
* TPS – Tokens Per Seconds (e.g. T2000 with RPS 2 yield TPS 4000\)

First we examine to prefill only case for saturation. The experiments series are split by the RPS. On the left graph we see that the saturation point is somewhere between input tokens lengths of 2000 and 3000\. The right graph shows the same experiments only the x axis is by TPS, showing that this metrics is more indicative. 

![A graph and chart of a graphAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image10]

The matching metrics of running and waiting requests (along the time of the experiment) are shown below, for 3000 and 4000 input token lengths. Clearly the 3000 case is beyond the saturation point and a waiting queue is growing:

![][image11]

Next we run a series of experiments to identify the exact saturation point between 2000 and 3000, this by the num of waiting requests, and show the corresponding prefill time and TTFT throughout each experiment:

![A graph of a graphAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image12]

We see that on T2800 the waiting queue is ‘exploiting’, and same is the TTFT, where the waiting queue becomes the dominant factor, instead of the prefill time.

We repeat now the same experiment where decode is combined with max output tokens of 300\.  We examining now another important metric – the KV cache usage percentage. An interesting insight, clearly seen from the graphs, is that it’s fully corelated with the num request running parameter, although being a value in the \[0,1\] range\!

The series split by RPS:

![A comparison of graphs and chartsAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image13]

The waiting queue in T3000, in contrast to T2000:

![A graph of a number of numbersAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image14]

And the intermediate series between 2000 and 2800, note the kv cache usage percentage metric presented with the num requests running and waiting graphs, and its strong correlation to num requests running. 

![A graph of a stock marketAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image15]

## The MaxTinB (max-num-batched-tokens) vLLM parameter

On prefill-only experiments,  the MaxTinB (max-num-batched-tokens) vLLM parameter provides a deterministic limit. E.g. if it is set to 65000, in experiment with T5000 (5000 input tokens in each request) a limit of 12-13 is imposed. When decode is combined, according to the vLLM scheduler, the ‘tokens budget’ for request doing a decode is 1 (when there is no speculative decode) rather then the input length, thus enabling much higher number of running requests, and the  MaxTinB cannot be used to limit this.  

You’d expect that increasing MaxTinB  would improve the TTFT by allowing more concurrently running requests,  however due the vLLM scheduling algorithm it’s not the case, as shown in the following graph – the TTFT in the experiments series with MaxTinB \= 128k is higher than the two series with 32k and 16k. The TTFT in each series is ascending (but with noise) with the number of output tokens.

![A graph of a graph with different colored linesAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image16]

## 

## Another look on TPS (Token Per Seconds) 

TPS is the multiplicity of T (input tokens in requests) and RPS (Requests Per Seconds).  The following graphs presents 2 ways of getting the same TPS  of 15000, one with T3000 requests and RPS of 5, the other with T5000 requests and RPS of 3:

![A graph of different sizes and colorsAI-generated content may be incorrect.][image17]

We can see that in this composition of experiment (Output tokens  20,   MaxTinB 65K), the requests’ lengths is the dominant parameter for the waiting queue and as a result, for the TTFT.
